content_markdown:
An insulated container of negligible mass contains a mass $2M$ of a liquid. A piece of a metal of mass $M$ is dropped into the liquid. The temperature of the liquid increases by $10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the temperature of the metal decreases by $80\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ in the same time.

What is $\frac{\text{specific heat capacity of the liquid}}{\text{specific heat capacity of the metal}}$?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: Using the principle of conservation of energy, the heat lost by the metal equals the heat gained by the liquid: $Q_{\text{metal}} = Q_{\text{liquid}}$. This gives $M \cdot c_{\text{metal}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{metal}} = (2M) \cdot c_{\text{liquid}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{liquid}}$. Substituting the values: $M \cdot c_{\text{metal}} \cdot 80 = 2M \cdot c_{\text{liquid}} \cdot 10$. Simplifying the equation: $80 \cdot c_{\text{metal}} = 20 \cdot c_{\text{liquid}}$. Therefore, $\frac{c_{\text{liquid}}}{c_{\text{metal}}} = \frac{80}{20} = 4$.
